canonical_summary:
NOAA's Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs (CRSRA) office, housed in NESDIS, licenses U.S. private Earth-observation systems under 15 CFR Part 960. The 2020 rewrite, published in the Federal Register on May 20, 2020 and effective July 20, 2020, replaced case-by-case license conditions with a three-tier framework codified at 15 CFR 960.6. Tier assignment turns on whether a system can collect unenhanced data substantially the same as data already available, with foreign availability defining the Tier 1 frontier, U.S.-only availability defining Tier 2, and genuinely novel capabilities defining Tier 3.

Tier 1 systems carry only the baseline conditions in 15 CFR 960.8. Tier 2 adds cybersecurity controls, resolved imaging consent rules for artificial resident space objects, and possible limited operations directives under 15 CFR 960.9. Tier 3 layers on temporary, custom conditions under 15 CFR 960.10 to address national security or foreign policy concerns. A statutory shot clock obligates the Secretary to issue a determination within 60 days of a completeness notification, with a deemed grant backstop in 960.7(c).

Twenty-three systems held Tier 3 licenses as of Q1 2023. NOAA eliminated 39 Tier 3 temporary conditions on July 19, 2023 after foreign capability in SAR, nighttime imaging, and shortwave infrared matured, demonstrating how 15 CFR 960.6(c) functions as the operative re-tiering provision.
h1: NOAA remote-sensing license tiers: the complete guide to 15 CFR Part 960
meta_title: NOAA Remote-Sensing License Tiers: 15 CFR Part 960 Guide
meta_description: Definitive guide to NOAA CRSRA Tier 1, 2, and 3 remote-sensing licenses under 15 CFR Part 960: criteria, conditions, operators, timelines, and the 2020 reform.

sections.heading: The three-tier framework under 15 CFR 960.6
paragraphs: The U.S. licensing regime for private remote-sensing space systems is administered by NOAA's Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs (CRSRA) office, a component of the National Environmental Satellite, Data, and Information Service (NESDIS) within the Department of Commerce. Authority flows from the Land Remote Sensing Policy Act of 1992 (51 U.S.C. 60101 et seq.) and is implemented through 15 CFR Part 960. The Department published the rewritten Part 960 in the Federal Register on May 20, 2020 (85 FR 30790) and it took effect on July 20, 2020.

The cornerstone of the 2020 regime is 15 CFR 960.6, which sorts every licensed system into Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3. Categorization is anchored to a single legal test: whether the applicant's system can collect unenhanced data substantially the same as unenhanced data already available, and from whom. Tier-specific conditions then attach in 15 CFR 960.8 (baseline), 15 CFR 960.9 (Tier 2 additions), and 15 CFR 960.10 (Tier 3 additions including temporary custom conditions).

The substantially the same test under 960.6 means one item is a market substitute for another, taking into account all applicable factors. For imagery these factors include spatial resolution, spectral bandwidth, number of imaging bands, temporal resolution, persistence of imaging, local time of imaging, and any geographic or other restrictions on the data. CRSRA publishes quarterly Foreign Availability Benchmarks on nesdis.noaa.gov that document the comparison set.
bullets: Tier 1 (15 CFR 960.6(a)): unenhanced data substantially the same as data available from entities not licensed under Part 960, such as foreign operators.
Tier 2 (15 CFR 960.6(b)): unenhanced data substantially the same as data available only from other Part 960 licensees.
Tier 3 (15 CFR 960.6(c)): unenhanced data not substantially the same as anything offered by any domestic or foreign entity.

Tier assignments persist until the Secretary, after consulting Defense and State as appropriate, finds the system belongs in a lower-numbered tier, or until the licensee files and receives a modification under 15 CFR 960.13.

sections.heading: Tier 1: criteria, conditions, and representative capabilities
paragraphs: A system falls into Tier 1 when comparable unenhanced data is already available from operators not licensed under Part 960, which in practice means foreign commercial operators. If a foreign provider already markets the same class of imagery, the U.S. applicant inherits the lightest regulatory burden the rule offers.

Tier 1 systems carry only the standard conditions in 15 CFR 960.8. Those baseline conditions require the licensee to operate the system as described in the application, notify NOAA of anomalies and dispositions, file annual compliance certifications, and comply with U.S. law including export controls. Tier 1 systems are explicitly not subject to limited operations directives and do not face the cybersecurity, ARSO imaging, or temporary condition layers reserved for higher tiers.

subsections.heading: What capabilities are typically Tier 1 today
bullets: Sub-meter electro-optical imagery at resolutions matched by Airbus Pleiades Neo (30 cm) and similar foreign systems.
Multispectral imagery in the 3 to 5 m range matched by SPOT, RapidEye, and the Korean CAS500 series.
Moderate-resolution panchromatic and multispectral collection matched by foreign benchmarks in the Q1 2024 Availability Benchmarks report.
Modalities that have been moved into Tier 1 over time as foreign capability advances, per 15 CFR 960.6(c).

sections.heading: Tier 2 and Tier 3: cybersecurity, ARSO imaging, and temporary conditions
paragraphs: Tier 2 captures systems whose unenhanced data is matched only by other Part 960 licensees. Tier 2 licensees comply with all 15 CFR 960.8 conditions plus the additions in 15 CFR 960.9. The principal additions are cybersecurity measures sufficient to prevent unauthorized access, the resolved imaging rule for artificial resident space objects (ARSOs), and compliance with any limited operations directive the Secretary may issue.

NOAA's Guidance Circular 960.9(a)-1 spells out the cybersecurity floor: NIST-approved encryption with at least a 256-bit key length for commands to on-orbit assets and for system data transmissions, plus industry best-practice controls. The resolved imaging rule (Guidance Circular 960.9(b)) requires written consent from the registered owner of any ARSO before imaging it at 3 by 3 pixels or greater, plus at least five days advance notice to the Secretary.

Tier 3 captures genuinely novel capabilities. A Tier 3 system must comply with 960.8, the 960.9 Tier 2 conditions, and the 960.10 Tier 3 additions. Section 960.10(b) authorizes the Secretary to impose custom temporary license conditions to address national security or foreign policy concerns specific to the novel capability. The rule directs the Secretary to notify the Secretaries of Defense and State of every Tier 3 categorization and to aim to craft the least restrictive temporary license conditions possible before the 60-day application review window closes.
bullets: Tier 2 cybersecurity floor: NIST-approved encryption with a 256-bit key length minimum (Guidance Circular 960.9(a)-1).
Resolved imaging trigger: any image of an ARSO at 3 by 3 pixels or greater requires owner consent and 5 days advance notice.
Tier 3 temporary conditions: bounded in time, calibrated to a typical three-year horizon, and subject to expiration if not renewed.
Limited operations directives can attach to Tier 2 or Tier 3 systems during heightened national security or international obligation concerns.

sections.heading: Application process and the 60-day shot clock
paragraphs: CRSRA encourages an informal pre-application touchpoint. Under 15 CFR 960.5(a) a prospective applicant may consult informally with the Secretary to discuss whether a license is likely to be required. NESDIS implements this through an Initial Contact Form, characterized as for informational purposes only and considered non-binding, and direct outreach to crsra@noaa.gov. There is no single PDF form; applications must be submitted in accordance with the specific instructions in Appendix B to Part 960 and contain fully accurate and responsive information as described in Appendix A.

After receipt, the Secretary has 7 days under 15 CFR 960.5(c) to determine whether the submission is complete. The 60-day adjudication clock in 15 CFR 960.7(b) starts only on the completeness notification. The Secretary must issue a written grant or denial within 60 days of that notification. A deemed grant backstop in 960.7(c) lets an applicant whose application has sat past 60 days file a request, after which the Secretary shall grant the license within 3 additional days absent specific, credible evidence of non-compliance.

Within the 60 days, NOAA does not act alone. Appendix D to Part 960 codifies a memorandum of understanding among the Department of Commerce, Department of State, Department of Defense, and Department of the Interior, with the Office of the Director of National Intelligence and the Joint Chiefs of Staff participating as supporting members. Review is staged through an Operating Committee with escalation to an Advisory Committee and a Review Board.

subsections.heading: Practical end-to-end timeline
bullets: Pre-application engagement: open-ended, does not count against statutory clocks.
Completeness review: up to 7 days under 15 CFR 960.5(c).
Adjudication: 60 days under 15 CFR 960.7(b).
Deemed grant: 3 days after a missed-deadline request under 15 CFR 960.7(c).
Safe planning assumption for first-time Tier 3 applicants: full 60 days plus pre-filing weeks.

sections.heading: Re-tiering: how 15 CFR 960.6(c) responds to foreign advancement
paragraphs: The operative provision that lets NOAA move an already issued license between tiers is 15 CFR 960.6(c). It provides that a system shall remain in the tier assigned until the Secretary, after consulting the Secretaries of Defense and State as appropriate, determines the system belongs in a lower-numbered tier due to the advancement of non-U.S. commercial remote sensing capabilities or due to other facts, or until the Secretary grants a licensee's modification request under 960.13.

CRSRA publishes quarterly Remote Sensing License Tiering benchmarks that summarize foreign availability across modalities such as panchromatic resolution, hyperspectral bands, X-band SAR, nighttime imaging, shortwave infrared, and non-Earth imaging. As foreign systems improve, the benchmark moves, and capabilities once Tier 3 or Tier 2 can become Tier 1. The CRSRA FAQ directly ties the benchmarks to 960.6(c).

The clearest public example came on July 19, 2023, when the first Tier 3 temporary conditions imposed under 15 CFR 960.10(b) at the start of the new regime permanently expired. CRSRA followed with license modifications across the Tier 3 portfolio to remove 39 individual temporary conditions. The action covered all X-band SAR temporary conditions, reduced global imaging restrictions to under 1 percent of Earth's surface for certain modes, and removed conditions on non-Earth imaging, rapid revisit, nighttime imaging, and shortwave infrared. CRSRA framed the action as a direct response to maturing foreign capabilities documented in its benchmarks.
bullets: 15 CFR 960.5(d) keeps facts about a system current during application.
15 CFR 960.6(c) lets NOAA respond when foreign capability outside the licensee changes.
July 19, 2023: 39 Tier 3 temporary conditions removed across the U.S. operator portfolio.
Each new quarterly benchmark is a standing invitation for re-tiering on the licensee's request or NOAA's own motion.

sections.heading: Current NOAA-licensed operators by tier
paragraphs: As of the first quarter of 2023, 23 systems held Tier 3 licenses under 15 CFR Part 960. The list below reflects each operator's most recent public tier categorization and capability set. Tier 2 assignments are rarely disclosed publicly because they trigger no public-facing temporary conditions.

subsections.heading: Tier 3 electro-optical and very-high-resolution
bullets: Maxar Intelligence: WorldView Legion plus legacy WorldView and GeoEye satellites; 30 cm-class electro-optical and stereo; longest-standing Tier 3 licensee, originally granted relief in June 2018 to sell 25 cm imagery.
Planet Labs: SuperDove (3 m multispectral) and SkySat (sub-50 cm electro-optical); Tier 3 for SkySat sub-meter capability; relief on July 19, 2023.
BlackSky: Gen-2 and Gen-3 electro-optical constellation with sub-meter resolution and high revisit; Tier 3 licensee per CRSRA reporting.

Albedo: 2024 Tier 3 license for 10 cm/pixel panchromatic and 4 m thermal, the first commercial authorization for that optical GSD, with conditions for sensitive sites, revisit limits, and encrypted downlink.

subsections.heading: Tier 3 synthetic aperture radar
bullets: Capella Space: U.S. X-band SAR constellation marketed at sub-0.25 m spotlight; all X-band SAR temporary conditions removed July 19, 2023.
ICEYE US: X-band SAR constellation down to 25 cm; direct beneficiary of the 2023 X-band SAR condition removals; NRO Sensor Strategic Commercial Enhancements contract reawarded in December 2024.
Umbra: X-band SAR; first U.S. operator publicly authorized to deliver 16 cm SAR imagery after July 19, 2023; NRO contract renewed December 2024.

subsections.heading: Tier 3 novel modalities
bullets: Pixxel: U.S.-incorporated entity; Tier 3 for Firefly hyperspectral constellation; 5 m GSD across 150+ bands with 40 km swath; first Firefly satellites launched on SpaceX Transporter-12 in January 2025.

HEO USA: first NOAA Tier 3 license authorizing a commercial optical camera in high LEO above 800 km for non-Earth imaging; Holmes Mk2 launched November 28, 2025 on Impulse Space's Mira as part of LEO Express-3.

subsections.heading: Assumed Tier 2
bullets: Satellogic: first NOAA license issued November 21, 2023 after Delaware redomicile; 36 sub-meter Aleph-1 satellites; sub-meter multispectral video and stills; tier not publicly disclosed but foreign equivalents (Pleiades Neo, CAS500) suggest Tier 2.

sections.heading: The 2020 reform: what changed and why it matters
paragraphs: The 2020 rewrite of 15 CFR Part 960, finalized in 85 FR 30790, was the most significant overhaul of U.S. commercial remote sensing licensing since the Land Remote Sensing Policy Act of 1992. It replaced a permissive but slow case-by-case regime, where bespoke license conditions targeted entire sensing phenomenologies, with a tier-based framework that pegs the level of U.S. regulation to foreign availability and reserves the most restrictive treatment for genuinely novel capabilities.

Three structural changes mattered most to industry. First, the rule eliminated the prior permanent operating conditions that had specifically targeted SAR, night-time imaging, and short-wave infrared sensors; those restrictions now attach only as Tier 3 temporary conditions or through limited operations directives. Second, the rule introduced a hard 60-day shot clock in 15 CFR 960.7(b) with a deemed grant backstop, compressing what had historically been an open-ended timeline. Third, the rule formalized re-tiering under 15 CFR 960.6(c) so that foreign advancement automatically pulls U.S. systems into lighter regimes.
bullets: Final rule published May 20, 2020 (85 FR 30790); effective July 20, 2020.
Three-tier framework codified in 15 CFR 960.6 replaces case-by-case conditions.
60-day adjudication clock with 7-day completeness review and 3-day deemed grant.
Permanent SAR, NTI, and SWIR restrictions removed from the rule itself.
Quarterly Foreign Availability Benchmarks drive ongoing re-tiering decisions.
First wave of Tier 3 condition removals: July 19, 2023, covering 39 individual temporary conditions.

faqs.question: What is the legal test for assigning a NOAA remote-sensing license to Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3?
faqs.answer: Under 15 CFR 960.6, the test is whether a system can collect unenhanced data substantially the same as data already available, and from whom. Tier 1 applies when comparable data is already available from operators not licensed under Part 960, such as foreign operators. Tier 2 applies when comparable data is available only from other Part 960 licensees. Tier 3 applies when comparable data is not available from any domestic or foreign entity. Substantially the same means one item is a market substitute for another, weighed against factors including spatial resolution, spectral bandwidth, number of imaging bands, temporal resolution, persistence, local time, and geographic restrictions.

faqs.question: What are the additional license conditions for Tier 2 systems under 15 CFR 960.9?
faqs.answer: Tier 2 licensees must comply with the baseline 15 CFR 960.8 conditions plus three additions. First, cybersecurity measures sufficient to prevent unauthorized access; NOAA's Guidance Circular 960.9(a)-1 calls for NIST-approved encryption with at least a 256-bit key length for commands to on-orbit assets and for system data transmissions. Second, the resolved imaging rule for artificial resident space objects: any image of an ARSO at 3 by 3 pixels or greater requires written consent from the registered owner and at least 5 days advance notice to the Secretary. Third, compliance with any limited operations directive the Secretary may issue.

faqs.question: How does NOAA categorize Tier 3 systems and what temporary conditions apply?
faqs.answer: A system is Tier 3 under 15 CFR 960.6(c) when it can collect unenhanced data not substantially the same as anything available from any domestic or foreign source. Tier 3 captures genuinely novel capabilities, whether new modality, new resolution class, or new collection geometry. Under 15 CFR 960.10, the Secretary may impose custom temporary license conditions to address national security or foreign policy concerns. The rule directs the Secretary to notify the Secretaries of Defense and State of every Tier 3 categorization and to aim to craft the least restrictive temporary license conditions possible before the 60-day review window closes.

faqs.question: How long does a NOAA CRSRA license application take from submission to decision?
faqs.answer: A clean filing tracks to roughly 67 days from receipt to grant. Under 15 CFR 960.5(c), the Secretary has 7 days to determine whether the application is complete. The 60-day adjudication clock in 15 CFR 960.7(b) starts only on the completeness notification, and the Secretary must issue a written grant or denial within 60 days. A deemed grant backstop in 15 CFR 960.7(c) lets the applicant request issuance after a missed deadline, with the Secretary required to grant the license within 3 additional days absent specific, credible evidence of non-compliance. Pre-application engagement does not count against either clock.

faqs.question: Which subsection of 15 CFR Part 960 allows NOAA to move a license between tiers after issuance?
faqs.answer: The operative provision is 15 CFR 960.6(c). It provides that a system shall remain in the tier assigned until the Secretary, after consulting the Secretaries of Defense and State as appropriate, determines the system belongs in a lower-numbered tier due to the advancement of non-U.S. commercial remote sensing capabilities or other facts, or until the Secretary grants a licensee's modification request under 15 CFR 960.13. A common framing point: 15 CFR 960.5(d) keeps facts about a system current during application, but 960.6(c) is the mechanism for re-tiering an already issued license.

faqs.question: What happened on July 19, 2023 with NOAA Tier 3 license conditions?
faqs.answer: On July 19, 2023, the first Tier 3 temporary conditions imposed under 15 CFR 960.10(b) when the 2020 rule took effect permanently expired. CRSRA followed with license modifications across the Tier 3 portfolio that removed 39 individual temporary conditions. The action covered all X-band SAR temporary conditions, eased rapid revisit limits, removed conditions on non-Earth imaging, and removed restrictions tied to nighttime imaging and shortwave infrared modalities. CRSRA framed the action as a direct response to maturing foreign SAR, NTI, and SWIR capabilities documented in its quarterly Foreign Availability Benchmarks. Affected licensees included BlackSky, Capella Space, ICEYE US, Planet, Pixxel, and Umbra.

faqs.question: Which commercial remote-sensing operators currently hold NOAA Tier 3 licenses?
faqs.answer: Publicly identified Tier 3 NOAA licensees include Maxar Intelligence (WorldView Legion at 30 cm), Planet Labs (SkySat sub-meter), BlackSky (Gen-2 and Gen-3 sub-meter electro-optical), Capella Space (X-band SAR at sub-0.25 m), ICEYE US (X-band SAR at 25 cm), Umbra (X-band SAR authorized to 16 cm after July 2023), Pixxel (Firefly hyperspectral at 5 m across 150+ bands), HEO USA (non-Earth imaging above 800 km), and Albedo (10 cm panchromatic and 4 m thermal, licensed in 2024). As of Q1 2023, NOAA reported 23 systems held Tier 3 licenses. Tier 2 assignments are rarely disclosed publicly.

faqs.question: What is the resolved imaging rule for artificial resident space objects (ARSOs)?
faqs.answer: Under 15 CFR 960.9, a Tier 2 or Tier 3 licensee that resolves another artificial resident space object must first obtain written consent from the registered owner of the ARSO and give the Secretary at least 5 days advance notice. Resolved imaging is defined as any image of an ARSO at 3 by 3 pixels or greater. The rule reflects long-standing concerns about uncontrolled imaging of foreign government satellites and active military spacecraft. HEO USA's November 2025 Holmes Mk2 launch on Impulse Space's Mira was the first NOAA-licensed non-Earth imaging payload authorized to operate above 800 km altitude.

faqs.question: What was the prior regime before the 2020 rewrite of 15 CFR Part 960?
faqs.answer: Before May 20, 2020, NOAA licensed private remote-sensing space systems through a case-by-case process that imposed bespoke permanent operating conditions targeting entire sensing phenomenologies, including synthetic aperture radar, night-time imaging, and shortwave infrared. There was no fixed adjudication clock and no formal mechanism that tied U.S. restrictions to global market availability. The 2020 final rule, published at 85 FR 30790 and effective July 20, 2020, replaced this with the three-tier framework in 15 CFR 960.6, the 60-day shot clock in 15 CFR 960.7, and the re-tiering provision in 15 CFR 960.6(c) that responds to foreign capability advancement.

faqs.question: What inter-agency review happens during the 60-day NOAA license adjudication?
faqs.answer: Appendix D to 15 CFR Part 960 codifies a memorandum of understanding governing the inter-agency review. The Department of Commerce administers the license through NOAA. The Department of State participates for foreign policy and international obligations. The Department of Defense participates for national security. The Department of the Interior is a principal reviewing party. The Office of the Director of National Intelligence and the Joint Chiefs of Staff participate as supporting members. Review is staged through an Operating Committee with escalation paths to an Advisory Committee on Commercial Remote Sensing and a Review Board if disagreements persist.

faqs.question: What cybersecurity standards does NOAA require for Tier 2 and Tier 3 systems?
faqs.answer: NOAA's Guidance Circular 960.9(a)-1 implements the cybersecurity requirement in 15 CFR 960.9 for Tier 2 systems, which Tier 3 systems must also meet. The guidance calls for NIST-approved encryption with at least a 256-bit key length for commands to on-orbit assets and for system data transmissions, plus industry best-practice controls covering access management, vulnerability management, incident response, and supply-chain integrity. The aim is to prevent unauthorized access to the system that could allow a third party to task the spacecraft, tamper with downlinked data, or otherwise interfere with the licensed operation. Tier 1 systems are not subject to this requirement.

faqs.question: How does CRSRA decide when foreign capability is mature enough to justify re-tiering?
faqs.answer: CRSRA publishes quarterly Foreign Availability Benchmarks on nesdis.noaa.gov, recently renamed Availability Benchmarks. Each report summarizes foreign capability across modalities including panchromatic resolution, hyperspectral bands, X-band SAR resolution, nighttime imaging, shortwave infrared, and non-Earth imaging. The benchmark sets the current Tier 1 frontier. The CRSRA FAQ ties the benchmarks directly to 15 CFR 960.6(c): systems may only be tiered with respect to capabilities already available from foreign competitors, and as the picture shifts, NOAA can re-tier a system on its own motion or in response to a licensee modification request under 15 CFR 960.13.
